topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the shared lab hummed with an irritating monotony. Elias stared at the blueprint spread across the table, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the laminated paper. He blinked, rereading the schematics for the 'Aetheric Resonator' for the fifth time. Then, he glanced over. Across the narrow workspace, Clara was tracing the exact same circuit lines with a fine-tipped pen, her brow furrowed in concentration. A sudden, cold wave washed over him, making his breath catch in his throat. He slowly lowered his gaze, his shoulders slumping as if a physical weight had settled upon him. "This can't be," he managed, his voice sounding thin and brittle. He pushed back from the table, the scrape of his chair echoing too loudly in the quiet room, and walked toward the door, needing air that didn't smell of ozone and shared, uncanny duplication.

[story 2]

My hands were shaking so badly I had to set the soldering iron down on the workbench with a clatter. I looked at the prototype humming softly on the bench—the casing, the wiring diagram, even the minuscule etching on the base plate. Then I looked up. Sarah was leaning over, examining the same component, her expression a mixture of intense focus and something else, something that made my stomach clench tight. I felt a heat rising in my cheeks, a prickly sensation that made me want to retreat. "Are you... are you working on the same thing?" I asked, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. Her head snapped up, and for a fleeting second, I saw a flicker of surprise, quickly masked by a practiced, neutral expression. She just offered a tight, unreadable smile, and I felt a profound sense of disorientation, as if the ground beneath my feet had suddenly shifted.

[story 3]

The silence in the workshop was oppressive, thick enough to choke on. I traced the familiar flow of data across my monitor, the lines of code perfectly mirroring the structure I had just finalized an hour ago. I stopped breathing for a moment, my gaze drifting across the room to find Ben. He was meticulously organizing his tools, his movements precise, almost ritualistic. When his eyes met mine, there was a hesitation there, a subtle tightening around his jaw that spoke volumes. I felt a sudden, sharp ache behind my eyes, a pressure building behind my sternum. "We've been... on this?" I whispered, the sound barely audible above the low thrum of the ventilation system. Ben slowly straightened up, his posture stiffening, and he didn't meet my eyes, instead focusing intently on a smudge on his workbench, his silence screaming louder than any accusation could have.
